Excited to announce that we've raised 62.7M$ at 1.04B$ valuation, led by Daniel Gross, along with Stan Druckenmiller, NVIDIA, Jeff Bezos, Tobi Lutke, Garry Tan, Andrej Karpathy, Dylan Field, Elad Gil, Nat Friedman, IVP, NEA, Jakob Uszkoreit, Naval Ravikant, Brad Gerstner, and Lip-Bu Tan. We will use the additional funding to grow our usage across consumers and knowledge workers in enterprises. For consumers: we have inked partnerships with Deutsche Telekom and Softbank to distribute Perplexity to ~116M users worldwide. We earlier announced a similar partnership with SK Telecom at the Mobile World Congress in February. And for Enterprise: we've announced the launch of Perplexity Enterprise Pro, to bring the world's best AI answer engine into the hands of every knowledge worker in every organization: https://lnkd.in/egwSgGJX Daniel is a special investor. I really look up to him and his career. He was a founder and worked on a search engine called Cue, and sold the company to Apple, where he ran Spotlight Search and several other projects. He was also the first real beta-tester of http://Perplexity.ai prior to its launch in Dec 2022. Thanks for all the help and support from all our users so far! We look forward to continuing to accelerate and grow and to providing accurate knowledge and information to every person on the planet. Blog: https://lnkd.in/e_BE-4Qi Bloomberg: https://lnkd.in/edpK28dp

I faced an impossible choice: 60 days to create a strategy for Ecuador's $3.5 billion in agricultural exports to Germany. 12 competing products. Limited budget. A small team. And a compliance deadline that could lock 120 producers out of the EU market. The traditional answer? A $500k, 8-week consulting report. I had neither the time nor the money. So I built a decision simulation engine called TACTIK. It’s not another chatbot. It’s a flight simulator for high-stakes conversations that integrates the conflicting advice of multiple experts—transparently. Then, the ultimate test: I ran a simulation of a trade crisis my father faced 30 years ago as Ambassador to France. The result? TACTIK matched his 30 years of diplomatic experience with 95% accuracy. 5moEdited
While I'm still coming closer to the UX itself, before it we have to understand the way how we proceed with the information. I talked about perception, now I want to talk about memory, which is important to keep in mind while you are thinking about an experience overall.
- Did you know that we forget about 40% of new information after only 20 minutes, and 70% just the next day?
- Or did you know that our brain can alter our memory? And we can remember things which never actually happened!
- Did you know that our working memory can hold about 4–5 items for adults and 2-3 items for children according to recent research?
- To remember something and transfer it to long-term memory, we have to record it in detail, through meaningful use or association.
- Also, emotion can make experiences and information more memorable.

Turns out, too many Agents spoil the broth, too! This week, I explored deep research agents, systems that simulate layered reasoning through iteration and reflection. I built a configurable multi-agent research agent, ran several configurations, and used an LLM-based evaluator to score their outputs. A single researcher agent iterating deeply performed better than multiple researcher agents working in parallel. Observations: - Deeper iteration builds a more coherent understanding. - Parallel agents explore more ideas, but they don’t reinforce or refine each other’s reasoning. - API rate limits wiped out most of the theoretical speed gains from parallel runs. How it works: A hierarchical supervisor–researcher setup: - The supervisor delegates sub-topics to researcher agents - Each researcher runs ReAct loops with web search and reflection tools - Configurable parameters control parallelism, iteration depth, and tool usage Video demo: https://lnkd.in/gS26pbnH Jupyter Notebook: https://lnkd.in/gs5NgW3a Thanks to AI Makerspace for another great week of learning!#AIE8

Today, we're launching the first version of Perplexity's Enterprise Search, called "Internal Knowledge Search". For a long time, people have wanted one single tool and platform to do all their research at work. And research isn't restricted to just knowledge that lies internally; you want the insights from the web in the context of what's most relevant to your organization. Yet, the tools that supported external search and internal search were fragmented, separate, and built on completely different legacy tech stacks; making it difficult to have one consolidated tool. With the advent of LLMs, multi-step reasoning, and tool use; it is now possible to address this issue and offer a fresh take on all research within an enterprise: be it internal files or external web, and we're glad to pioneer this at Perplexity. Enterprise is not just about file access, but also collaboration and customization. We're also excited to launch "Perplexity Spaces", a simple way for you build research hubs where you can upload persistent files (to have them as additional sources), pick an AI model of your choice, write custom instructions for the type of response you want, and invite collaborators to share your threads. Collaboratively picking which files to add to a space lets you address a big issue in Enterprise Search: "ranking" - which files actually matter for the company; and we believe users picking them is a solid way to address duplication, incomplete / incorrect / outdated documents etc that aren't addressed with connectors. This feature is also available to regular users who want to build custom spaces for repeat use cases of Perplexity. Spaces is already available on the sidebar to all users; and existing Enterprise users should already be able to toggle between Web & Org mode for their sources and start uploading files on their Settings tab! Check out our blog post to learn more: https://pplx.ai/ieEXvh4

The current wave in AI isn’t about smarter chatbots — it’s about agentic systems: platforms not only generating content but acting autonomously to deliver outcomes. These are not assistants, they are co-builders of worlds — ecosystems where human vision is amplified through machine agency. A recent article on designing successful agentic AI systems reinforces three pivotal truths: 1. Define the world you’re building. Agentic systems require more than good models; they need a clear environment, goals and constraints. Without a defined “world” for the agent to inhabit, autonomy becomes confusion, not value. 2. Calibrate human-machine roles. When agencies drift, humans retreat into monitoring or abdication. To avoid this, define the ledger of who initiates, who oversees, who escalates. Designers must build human-agent teams where agency is shared yet accountable. 3. Governance is not an add-on—it’s the scaffolding. Autonomous action without guardrails is not freedom, it’s risk. As systems scale, governance must scale too: decision-rights, escalation paths, feedback loops, and ethical design become core architecture, not just legal compliance. In many organizations I traverse, the temptation is to treat “agentic AI” as a feature: a fancy button, a proof-of-concept. But that framing misses the magnitude. You’re not tweaking a tool: you’re constructing a new operational domain. That means building worlds in which people and machines co-inhabit — where machines act, humans guide, and the shared purpose is clear. Here’s a checklist for leaders who intend to be builders of worlds: • Vision anchor: What future state is the system enabling? Describe the world. • Role clarity: Who does what? Humans + agents + hybrid workflows. • Outcome metrics: How will success manifest in the world you’re creating? • Feedback infrastructure: What loops bring signals back into design? • Ethical & governance layer: What rights, risks, and remediation flows exist? • Change cadence: How will you iterate the world as agents learn? If your answer to “What world are we building?” is unclear, you shouldn’t build an agentic system yet. Because the inevitable result of mis-designed agency is another silo, another hidden cost, another fractured workflow. But if you get this right — you become not just a leader of projects — you become a world-builder. One who shapes the operating domain, aligns human and machine agency, and delivers a new terrain of value. Leadership today is not only about decisions made — it’s about domains created. And in the age of agentic AI, the domains we build will determine who leads.#AgenticAI#SystemsThinking#Leadership#OrganizationalTransformation#Innovation#AIstrategy#HumanMachineTeam#Design#FutureOfWork

Aravind Srinivas, CEO of Perplexity, had one goal when he got into IIT Madras: to study Computer Science. In his first semester, he pushed himself to achieve the grades needed to switch from Electrical Engineering to his dream field. But when results came, he missed the cutoff by just 0.01 points. Feeling defeated, Aravind found himself slipping into a shell, feeling like he didn’t quite belong. But instead of giving up, he threw himself into learning on his own. He discovered online programming courses and taught himself Python before it was even popular at IIT. It became a way to prove, even if only to himself, that he could do it. By his third year, he topped a tough machine learning course, surprising even his professors. His hard work paid off when AI pioneer Yoshua Bengio offered him an internship, eventually recommending him for a PhD. Aravind was accepted at UC Berkeley, where his work caught the attention of John Schulman at OpenAI. That led to an internship at OpenAI, a dream come true. After stints at DeepMind and Google, he returned to OpenAI, but soon decided to forge his own path. In 2022, he launched Perplexity AI, which is now valued at over $8B. That missed 0.01% in college had lit a fire that took him far beyond his dreams. If you love something, your coursework doesn't matter. You can still make it big.

Here's a small test project I created as part of a technical assessment for a startup. Built using .NET 8 and C# with the MVC framework, this is a tipping system designed for businesses to manage and calculate daily tips based on employee shifts in a specific roster.
The project leverages the strategy pattern, a widely recognized design pattern in the industry for its flexibility and runtime interchangeability of algorithms.

5mo
At 19 years old, I wasn’t like most teenagers. Instead of college dreams or carefree days, I was in shackles, being transported from Mexico to the United States. The kind of transfer reserved for dangerous criminals—armed guards escorting me off a plane like a threat to society. A kid, in their eyes, but to them, I was something else.
I’ll never forget the cold stares of the officers, the way they looked at me as if I were a monster, not a young man who had barely started living. That walk, under the watchful eyes of rifles and handguns, wasn’t just a physical journey across borders. It was a walk into a dark place in my mind, one where I started to believe that this is who I was supposed to be. It was as if every click of those shackles was sealing a part of my fate.
Imagine what it does to a 19-year-old kid’s mind—to be treated like an animal, like a danger that needs to be contained. It felt like I was stepping into a role they’d written for me, and I had no choice but to play it out. The hardest part wasn’t the flight or the guards—it was fighting the idea that this was all I was ever going to be.
Today, I share this not to glorify the past, but to show you how deep the scars can go and how far I’ve had to come. It’s easy to look at where I am now and think it was always this way, but it wasn’t. It took years of hard work to rewrite the narrative they tried to stamp on my life at 19.
From walking in chains to walking free, building a legacy of change.
